title: Workshop: Open Economy, Tariffs, and the Exchange Rate
summary: Work through how trade policy and macro shocks move the real exchange rate. Use the chart's tariff lever and savings-shock controls.

questions.prompt: The fundamental identity NX = S - I. If a country's investment opportunities improve (think US shale boom, late 2000s), what happens to NX in the medium run? How does the dollar adjust?
questions.notes: Higher I → S < I → NX < 0 (current account deficit). Dollar appreciates to suck in foreign capital. The 'twin deficit' framing is partial — investment-driven deficits are different from consumption/fiscal-driven ones.

questions.prompt: A protective tariff is imposed at any given exchange rate ε. Using the NX vs ε chart, predict: does the equilibrium quantity of NX change? Does ε change? Does the trade balance 'improve'?
questions.notes: NX doesn't change in equilibrium (still pinned down by S - I). ε falls (real dollar appreciates). Trade balance is unchanged — tariffs don't fix it because the fundamental constraint is S - I. Households lose: imports cost more without offsetting export gains.

questions.prompt: China runs a structural current account surplus. By the NX = S - I identity, what's true of their S and I? Compare with a typical OECD country.
questions.notes: China S > I → NX > 0. Very high household saving + corporate retained earnings. OECD typically S ≈ I or S < I (consumer/fiscal-led economies). This is one frame for the 'savings glut' hypothesis.

questions.prompt: If the Fed cuts rates while the ECB holds steady, what should happen to the EUR/USD exchange rate (in the short run)? Use the interest parity intuition.
questions.notes: Capital flows toward higher-yielding EUR assets. EUR appreciates, USD depreciates. Lower US rates → cheaper dollar → more competitive US exports → NX up. (Long run still pinned by S - I.)

questions.prompt: Critique this claim: 'A weaker dollar always helps American exporters.' Identify at least two mechanisms that complicate it.
questions.notes: Counter-mechanisms: (1) imported inputs cost more (US firms use foreign intermediates); (2) commodity prices spike for US consumers; (3) global slowdown response makes foreign demand inelastic to FX; (4) pass-through can be slow and incomplete.

questions.prompt: Apply NX = S - I to the post-2017 US tariff escalation. Tariffs raised the cost of imports but the US trade deficit kept widening. Why is that consistent with the model?
questions.notes: US national S was falling (tax cuts, increased fiscal deficits). With S - I shrinking (more negative), NX must shrink (more negative) regardless of tariff policy. Tariffs change *composition* of imports and prices but not aggregate trade balance.
